summary: Problem 7 asks whether a uniform lattice Γ in a real semi-simple group containing 2-torsion can be the fundamental group of a closed manifold whose universal cover is ℚ-acyclic. The expected answer is 'No', supported by a short Euler-characteristic obstruction: a 2-torsion element acts freely on the ℚ-acyclic universal cover, which has Euler characteristic 1, yielding a contradiction. No complete written proof currently exists in the repository — the only issue is a closed placeholder seed task.
problems: No actual proof has been authored; the sole tracked issue is a seed/placeholder that was closed without an artifact to evaluate.
The 'best proof' record is inconsistent (status shows 100 open issues while only 1 issue is listed and resolved), indicating metadata/tracking unreliability.

The sketched obstruction is incomplete: it asserts a 2-torsion element acts freely on ~M but does not justify why the deck action is free (a finite-order deck transformation of a contractible/acyclic cover need not be free without additional argument).

The Euler characteristic argument needs rigor: ℚ-acyclic gives χ(~M)=1, but the divisibility/Lefschetz step linking free ℤ/2 action to a contradiction must be stated precisely (e.g., χ(~M) must be divisible by the group order for a free action on a finite ℚ-acyclic complex).
Compactness/finiteness hypotheses (closed manifold, finite-dimensionality of ~M) need to be invoked explicitly to apply Euler characteristic and Lefschetz fixed-point arguments.
highlights: A clear, plausible answer ('No') is identified with a concise conceptual obstruction via Euler characteristics and free actions.
Solvability is assessed at 58%, reflecting a genuinely tractable problem with a known elegant route.
The seed issue is resolved/closed, so there is no blocking error in the tracker — the path is open for a fresh, clean proof write-up.

suggested_todos.title: Draft a complete proof: show the 2-torsion deck transformation acts freely on ~M and derive the χ(~M)=1 contradiction via Lefschetz/Euler divisibility
Rigorously justify the freeness of the ℤ/2 deck action (manifold cover, fixed-point-free property of deck transformations)
State and apply the precise lemma: a finite group acting freely on a finite ℚ-acyclic complex forces |G| to divide χ, contradicting χ=1
Reconcile metadata inconsistencies (100 open issues vs 1 resolved issue) and attach the new proof artifact to a tracked issue for evaluation
Add a remark situating the result in the literature (Borel-type Euler characteristic / lattice torsion obstructions) for context and verification
